patient.communicationStyle: Confused and lethargic. Answers are vague or off-topic. Daughter at bedside provides most of the history. Patient may not report classic UTI symptoms.

responses.opening: (Daughter speaks) My mom is 78 and she's been acting really confused for the last two days. This isn't like her — she's normally sharp. She's had a fever and hasn't been eating or drinking. She fell yesterday trying to get to the bathroom.
responses.severity: (Patient) I'm fine... where am I? (Daughter) She keeps saying she's fine but she didn't know what day it was this morning.
responses.onset: (Daughter) She was fine on Monday. By Wednesday she seemed tired and wasn't making sense. The fever started yesterday. Today she couldn't get out of bed.
responses.urinarySymptoms: (Patient, when asked specifically) It burns a little when I go. I've been going a lot. (Daughter) She's been going to the bathroom every hour and she had an accident yesterday, which never happens.
responses.backPain: (Patient) My back hurts, down low. (Points to left flank.)
responses.mentalStatus: (Daughter) She keeps repeating herself. She asked me the same question five times this morning. She thought my son was my father.

chiefComplaint.location: Left flank discomfort (if asked); may not volunteer
chiefComplaint.onset: Progressive confusion over 2-3 days, fever for 1 day
chiefComplaint.collateralHistory: Daughter reports: confusion, anorexia, fever, urinary frequency, fall, incontinence

present.name: acute confusion/delirium
present.patientSays: What? I'm fine. What day is it?

present.name: fever/chills
present.patientSays: (Daughter) She's been burning up. Rigors last night — she was shaking.

present.name: urinary frequency
present.patientSays: I have to go all the time.

present.name: anorexia
present.patientSays: (Daughter) She hasn't eaten in two days. Maybe a few sips of water.

present.name: fall
present.patientSays: (Daughter) She fell yesterday getting up from her chair to go to the bathroom.

present.name: urinary incontinence (new)
present.patientSays: (Daughter) She had an accident yesterday. That's never happened before.

absent.name: recent hospitalization
absent.patientSays: (Daughter) No, she hasn't been in the hospital recently.

pmh: Recurrent UTI (3 in the past year)
Mild cognitive impairment (baseline — but this confusion is acute and much worse)

medications.name: Glipizide
medications.patientSays: (Daughter) She takes this for her sugar.

socialHistory.livingSituation: Lives alone. Daughter checks on her daily. Independent at baseline with IADLs.
socialHistory.functionalStatus: Independent at baseline — drives, cooks, manages medications. This is a significant change.

present: diabetes (impairs immune function and bladder emptying)

physicalExam.general: Elderly woman appearing acutely ill, lethargic, intermittently confused and agitated, dry mucous membranes
physicalExam.heent: Dry mucous membranes, no oral lesions, no neck stiffness
physicalExam.abdominal: Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness. No rebound or guarding. Bowel sounds present.
physicalExam.extremities: Mottled lower extremities, cool distal, delayed capillary refill (4 seconds). No edema.
physicalExam.neurologic: CAM-positive (acute onset, fluctuating, inattentive, disorganized thinking). Oriented to name only. No focal deficits. No meningismus.
physicalExam.skin: Warm trunk, cool/mottled extremities. No rash.

labs.note: Elevated — tissue hypoperfusion. Meets sepsis criteria.
Elevated from baseline of 0.9 — acute kidney injury (prerenal vs sepsis-related)
Elevated, BUN/Cr ratio >20 suggests prerenal component
Markedly elevated — strongly suggests bacterial infection

labs.value: Positive nitrites, positive LE, >100 WBC/hpf, many bacteria, moderate blood
labs.note: Pyuria with bacteriuria — consistent with UTI as source
Drawn before antibiotics — results typically 24-48 hours

imaging.findings: Left kidney appears edematous with perinephric fat stranding. No hydronephrosis. No obstructing stone. Small amount of perinephric fluid. Bladder wall thickening.
imaging.impression: Acute left pyelonephritis. No obstructing stone or abscess.

onCorrectTreatment.vitalsTrend: BP improves to 105/65 after 2L NS bolus. HR decreases to 90s. Lactate clears to 2.1 at 6 hours. Temp trends down.
onCorrectTreatment.symptomsTrend: Mental status gradually clearing over 24-48 hours. Eating and drinking by day 2. Creatinine improving.
onCorrectTreatment.narrative: 30mL/kg crystalloid bolus initiated. Blood and urine cultures obtained. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics started within 1 hour (ceftriaxone — avoiding fluoroquinolones given age/tendon risk). Foley placed for accurate I/Os — 200mL residual. Glucose managed with insulin sliding scale. Metformin held. Serial lactates clearing. Cultures grow E. coli sensitive to ceftriaxone. Antibiotics narrowed. ICU observation for 24 hours then step-down.

onDelayedTreatment.vitalsTrend: MAP drops to <65 despite fluids, lactate rises to 6.5, develops multiorgan dysfunction
complications: Acute kidney injury requiring potential dialysis
onDelayedTreatment.narrative: Progressive hemodynamic instability. Norepinephrine started. Repeat lactate 6.5. Anuric. ICU admission for vasopressor-dependent septic shock.

keyLearningPoints: Sepsis-3: suspected infection + SOFA score ≥2 (or qSOFA ≥2 for screening: altered mentation, RR≥22, SBP≤100)
In elderly patients, confusion may be the ONLY presenting symptom of serious infection
Hour-1 Sepsis Bundle: lactate, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4
UTI is the most common source of sepsis in elderly women
Diabetes is a major risk factor for complicated UTI — impairs immune function and bladder emptying
New confusion in elderly = delirium until proven otherwise. Always look for the underlying cause.
mustNotMiss: Meningitis (especially if neck stiffness or severe headache)
Obstructing ureteral stone with pyonephrosis (check for hydronephrosis on imaging)
Spinal epidural abscess (if back pain + neurologic deficits)
commonPitfalls: Attributing confusion to dementia without workup
Not checking a lactate in an ill-appearing elderly patient
Delaying antibiotics for imaging — antibiotics within 1 hour is the target
Inadequate fluid resuscitation — these patients are often profoundly volume-depleted
Using fluoroquinolones as first-line in elderly (tendon rupture, QTc prolongation, C. diff risk)

expertDifferential.why: Fever + confusion + urinary symptoms + pyuria + CVA tenderness + elevated lactate + sepsis physiology
Common in elderly, but clear lungs and no cough argue against
Always consider polypharmacy and metabolic derangements, but fever and lactate point to infection
Fever + confusion, but no neck stiffness and urinary source identified
Fever + abdominal tenderness, but suprapubic location and UA findings point to urinary source

teachingHooks.ifCollectsHistoryFromPatient: This patient is a poor historian due to delirium. Who else can give you the story? Always seek collateral from family, caregivers, or EMS.
teachingHooks.ifFocusesOnDementia: This is not her baseline. The daughter says she's normally sharp and independent. Acute change in mental status in the elderly = delirium, not worsening dementia.
teachingHooks.ifDelaysAntibiotics: Every hour of delay in antibiotics in sepsis increases mortality. What's your threshold for starting empiric treatment?
teachingHooks.ifMissesLactate: Lactate is your window into tissue perfusion. A normal blood pressure doesn't mean adequate perfusion — lactate may be elevated even with a 'normal' BP.
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutFluidVolume: 30mL/kg is the initial target. For a 70kg patient, that's 2.1L. Give it over the first 1-3 hours. Reassess and give more if needed.
